title: Household Survey Shows Reported and Expected Household Spending Growth Continue to Moderate

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: The December 2023 Survey of Consumer Expectations shows median monthly household spending growth declined to 5.0% (from 7.1% a year prior), while year-ahead spending expectations fell to 3.0%, the lowest since December 2020. Notably, households report they would allocate 38.4% of an unexpected 10% income increase to debt paydown (highest since 2016), while only 16.0% would be spent or donated (lowest since the series began in 2015).
key_points: Median reported monthly household spending growth declined to 5.0% in December 2023, from 5.5% in August and 7.1% in December 2022, remaining above the pre-pandemic level of 2.5%
Year-ahead expected overall household spending growth decreased to 3.0% in December 2023, from 3.4% in August, its lowest reading since December 2020

When receiving an unexpected 10% income increase, households report 38.4% would go to debt paydown (highest since August 2016), 45.6% to saving/investing, and only 16.0% to spending (lowest since series began in 2015)
Share of households reporting at least one large purchase in the past four months fell to 59.7% from 63.5% in August 2023
Median year-ahead expected growth in essential spending dropped to 4.5% (lowest since December 2020), while non-essential spending expectations remained unchanged at 1.9%
Large purchase intentions declined for home appliances, electronics, furniture, home repairs, vehicles, and vacations; only home-buying intentions increased
why_it_matters: This NY Fed survey provides a forward-looking indicator of consumer behavior that feeds into Fed policy calculations. The continued moderation in spending expectations and the increased propensity to pay down debt rather than spend suggest households are becoming more cautious, which could impact the pace of disinflation andFed rate cut deliberations.
macro_context: The survey data is directly relevant to Fed policy because it shows disinflationary pressure from demand-side moderation. Declining spending growth expectations (3.0% vs 5.5% reported) suggest the labor market-driven consumption boom is fading. The shift toward debt paydown rather than spending when receiving unexpected income (38.4% vs historical ~18% spent) indicates structural household conservatism that could slow consumption recovery even if incomes improve.
important_numbers: 38.4% would pay down debt from unexpected income increase
16.0% would be spent or donated from unexpected income increase
59.7% reported making at least one large purchase in past four months
notes: Historical record (published Jan 2024, over 26 months before TODAY). Confidence lowered accordingly. Source is a NY Fed research blog post release of the SCE Household Spending Survey for December 2023. Reclassified topic from 'market structure' (per metadata) to 'macro catalysts' as this source contains consumer spending survey data, not market structure content. No direct market impact described in source; asset class impact marked unknown.
